retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial hyperplasia or malignancy : Endometrial hyperplasia is classified as benign endometrial hyperplasia or intraepithelial neoplasia. Management of hyperplasia consists of surgical and nonsurgical therapies; the treatment approach is individualized based on clinical factors (eg, comorbid conditions). However, endometrial carcinoma requires surgical treatment and staging by gynecologic oncology specialists. Additionally, chemotherapy and radiation may be indicated for some patients. [18] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Biopsy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for endometrial biopsy include: Abnormal uterine bleeding Evaluation for endometrial neoplasia or precancerous hyperplasia Surveillance of previously diagnosed endometrial hyperplasia or cancer Evaluation of uterine response to hormone therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Etiology -- Endogenous Sources
retrieved_docs.content: Late menopause, usually described in those aged 55 and older

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Benign endometrial hyperplasia : Also referred to as nonatypical endometrial hyperplasia, this type of hyperplasia is typically managed with hormonal therapy and/or curettage. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The most recent clinical guidelines recommend that in women with endometrial hyperplasia who undergo medical management, an endometrial biopsy should be undertaken at least every 3 months until 2 consecutive negative biopsies are obtained, especially in patients with atypical endometrial hyperplasia. [21] More extensive evaluations, such as sentinel lymph node assessment in patients with atypical endometrial hyperplasia, have not been shown to be beneficial in current studies and are not recommended. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If fertility preservation is not desired, total hysterectomy is generally recommended for women with atypical endometrial hyperplasia due to the presumed significant risk of concurrent future endometrial cancer and for women with persistent non–atypical endometrial hyperplasia. [37] Postmenopausal women should be offered a total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophrectomy. Accurately quantifying endometrial cancer risk in women diagnosed with endometrial hyperplasia is, therefore, pivotal to informed shared decision-making regarding the most appropriate clinical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Biopsy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There is another special subset of pre and postmenopausal women in whom the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists provide specific guidance in their recommendations for endometrial biopsy. This includes women who are on tamoxifen therapy. An endometrial biopsy should be performed for all postmenopausal patients on tamoxifen therapy who have abnormal uterine bleeding. It is common for premenopausal women to experience menstrual irregularities, most commonly amenorrhea, while taking tamoxifen. The guidelines do not suggest routine endometrial biopsy in these women. Unless there are risk factors for endometrial cancer, premenopausal women treated with tamoxifen do not require any additional screening or workup beyond routine gynecologic care. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Managing endometrial hyperplasia effectively often involves a multidisciplinary approach, with various specialists collaborating to tailor treatment plans to each patient's unique needs and fertility objectives. Consultations that may be necessary to ensure thorough evaluation, personalized management, and optimal outcomes for individuals affected by endometrial hyperplasia include the following: Gynecologist Gynecologic oncologist Endocrinologist Fertility specialist Radiologist Pathologist Nutritionist Psychologist

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: According to the American College of Obstetrics and Gynecologists (ACOG), the management plan for patients diagnosed with premalignant endometrial lesions includes: [24] Ruling out the concurrent presence of endometrial carcinoma Strategic management plan designed to accommodate late diagnosis of occult carcinoma Prevention of progression to endometrial cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of benign endometrial hyperplasia without atypia progressing to invasive malignancy is less than 5% over 10 years. [31] Endometrial hyperplasia (and even low-grade endometrioid cancers) can be conservatively managed by progestin therapies (eg, oral or intrauterine progestogens), especially among women who wish to maintain fertility. Spontaneous resolution can occur if reversible causes of estrogen excess are corrected. [29] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Evaluation -- Endometrial Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Postmenopausal bleeding : Any vaginal bleeding after menopause warrants evaluation due to the increased risk of endometrial cancer. [28] Abnormal uterine bleeding in premenopausal women : Persistent or irregular bleeding patterns, especially in women 35 or older or premenopausal women with risk factors (eg, nulliparity, history of polycystic ovary syndrome, tamoxifen use, or family history of Lynch syndrome or hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer, or unopposed estrogen use) or persistent or recurrent AUB despite treatment, should prompt endometrial assessment to exclude hyperplasia, malignancy, and other differential diagnoses. [28] [29] Abnormal endometrial findings on imaging : Suspicious findings on transvaginal ultrasound (eg, thickened endometrium greater than or equal to 4 mm in postmenopausal women or focal endometrial thickening in premenopausal women) warrant a biopsy. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of endometrial hyperplasia and its implications for progression to endometrial cancer. Apply strategies for evaluating endometrial hyperplasia, including the use of appropriate diagnostic tools such as transvaginal ultrasound and endometrial sampling. Select evidence-based treatment modalities for endometrial hyperplasia, differentiating instances in which each would be appropriate. Implement effective collaboration among interprofessional team members to identify patients with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia, promoting counseling on lifestyle modifications for risk reduction and facilitating early therapeutic interventions to decrease progression to malignancy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Progesterone acts as a counterbalance to the mitogenic effects of estrogens. It induces secretory differentiation of the glandular epithelium. [25] Progesterone is normally well-tolerated and with side effects e.g., infrequent edema, gastrointestinal disturbances that are well tolerated. However, occasionally it may lead to thromboembolic events. It is also a reasonable option for any patient with a hyperplastic or pre-cancerous lesion who desires uterine retention. It can also be used for most elderly patients with medical comorbidities with either premalignant endometrial hyperplasia or low-grade malignancy. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial polyps : Thirty percent of PMB cases are caused by endometrial polyps; however, polyps can be asymptomatic. Furthermore, the severity of PMB is not affected by the number or size of polyps. One percent of all endometrial polyps are malignant, most commonly occurring in postmenopausal women. [16] Therefore, endometrial polyps in symptomatic postmenopausal women should be removed and histologically assessed. Surgical excision should be considered in asymptomatic women at higher risk of malignancy (eg, large polyps, tamoxifen use, obesity, or diabetes). [37] Hysteroscopic polypectomy is the preferred treatment because the clinician can obtain directed biopsies and excise polyps at once. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Hyperplasia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have found high rates of disease regression in patients who decide against surgical management and, instead, are managed medically. A systematic review of outcomes in patients with endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia–atypical endometrial hyperplasia or grade 1 adenocarcinoma demonstrated an initial response to progestins of 86%, with a complete response of 66% for those with atypical endometrial hyperplasia. Oral, intrauterine, and combined modes of administration are effective. Recent studies have identified complete response rates of 43% and 82% for endometrial adenocarcinoma and atypical endometrial hyperplasia, respectively, after 6 months of treating these conditions with the levonorgestrel IUD. [40]

retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate is indicated as a possible treatment for menopausal disorders, while synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are each identified as potential therapies for alleviating menopausal symptoms. These estrogenic agents represent therapeutic options that can be employed to manage the various clinical manifestations associated with menopause, providing clinicians with multiple pharmacologic choices to address both the broader disorder and its specific symptomatic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone, a synthetic progestogenic compound, may be used to treat انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometrial hyperplasia). By supplying progestin activity, 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone counteracts estrogen‑driven proliferation of the uterine lining, helping to normalize endometrial thickness and lower the risk of progression to malignancy. This therapeutic application highlights the role of hormonal modulation in managing intrauterine disorders and underscores the importance of appropriate hormonal therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate and synthetic conjugated estrogens, A are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing estrogen replacement to alleviate symptoms such as hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal atrophy, and other manifestations of estrogen deficiency associated with menopause. Their therapeutic application underscores the importance of estrogen therapy in managing the clinical features of menopausal transition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1407
retrieved_docs.content: Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain constitute a class of estrogenic compounds that includes estradiol as a member. Both estradiol and the agent termed estrogeny are indicated for the treatment of menopausal disorders, highlighting their therapeutic role in alleviating menopause‑related symptoms. Recognizing estradiol’s membership in the broader natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate estrogen therapy for managing menopausal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 654
retrieved_docs.content: ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE is a hormone replacement product formulated as a 28‑tablet pack (Prempro 0.625/2.5 28 Day) that contains conjugated estrogens 0.625 mg and medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg per tablet. The tablets deliver the active moiety of medroxyprogesterone acetate together with the estrogen component, providing both progestogenic and estrogenic activity in a single, fixed‑dose combination. This product is classified as a member of “Progestogens and estrogens, fixed combinations,” a pharmacologic class that is a specific type of “progestogens and estrogens in combination.” Recognizing ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE as part of this class helps clinicians understand that it supplies a predetermined ratio of estrogen and progestogen, which is important for managing menopausal symptoms and for protecting the endometrium from hyperplasia when estrogen therapy is used.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol and estrogeny are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for alleviating the symptoms associated with the menopausal transition. Both substances are forms of estrogen therapy and are considered in clinical management to improve quality of life for patients experiencing menopausal-related changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine/endometrial disorders are classified as a subset within the broader category of enlarged uterine leiomyoma tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), indicating that conditions affecting the uterus and endometrium are encompassed by the pathology of large fibroid growths. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing fibroid enlargement as a central component in the spectrum of uterine/endometrial disease and suggests that assessment of fibroid size and impact is a key aspect of managing patients with uterine/endometrial disorders.
